name: Argentina (1829–1835)

colors.symbolism: the deep blue (azul oscuro) of the Federalist faction under Rosas
the rays of the Sun of May
outline of the Sun of May features

description: Three horizontal stripes of equal width — dark blue, white, dark blue — with a yellow Sun of May (a human face surrounded by 32 alternating straight and wavy rays) centred on the white stripe.
history: Used during the first governorship of Juan Manuel de Rosas in Buenos Aires Province and as the de facto national flag of the Argentine Confederation. Following Belgrano's blue-white-blue cockade flag of 1812 and the addition of the Sun of May in 1818, the Federalist administrations darkened the celestial blue to an almost indigo "federalist" shade as a partisan signal against the lighter blue preferred by the Unitarians. The dark-blue version was prevalent from roughly 1829 until 1835, when Rosas, returning to power with extraordinary faculties, replaced the sun with red and modified the flag further.

features.symbolism: the celestial blue and white of the cockade adopted by Manuel Belgrano in 1812; under the Federalist governor Juan Manuel de Rosas the blue was darkened to a deep "federalist" indigo to distinguish it from the lighter Unitarian shade
the sun that, according to legend, broke through the clouds during the May Revolution of 1810; the Inca sun-god Inti, invoking pre-colonial Andean heritage
